news_summary: Innovative efforts are enhancing accessibility to cultural heritage, strengthening support systems for domestic violence survivors, and reforming justice by reducing short jail terms.
news_summary_fr: Des initiatives novatrices améliorent l'accès au patrimoine culturel, renforcent les dispositifs d'aide aux victimes de violences conjugales et réforment le système judiciaire en réduisant la durée des peines d'emprisonnement de courte durée.
news_summary_es: Las iniciativas innovadoras están mejorando el acceso al patrimonio cultural, reforzando los sistemas de apoyo a las víctimas de violencia doméstica y reformando el sistema judicial mediante la reducción de las penas de prisión de corta duración.

articles.title: Naples lets blind visitors feel the Veiled Christ
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM On a Tuesday morning in Naples, a guide named Chiara Locovardi ran her gloved fingers across a marble surface that has baffled art historians for more than two centuries. She was describing what she felt to the group around her, all of them blind or partially sighted, all of […]
The post Naples lets blind visitors feel the Veiled Christ first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

On a Tuesday morning in Naples, a guide named Chiara Locovardi ran her gloved fingers across a marble surface that has baffled art historians for more than two centuries. She was describing what she felt to the group around her, all of them blind or partially sighted, all of them touching the Veiled Christ for the first time.

“The veil covering Christ is extraordinary,” Locovardi said. “It’s impossible to understand how Sanmartino managed to create it. The veil defies explanation — for those who can see and for those who cannot. When you touch it, you can feel the veins pulsing beneath.”

The event, called La meraviglia a portata di mano (Wonder within reach), was organized by the museum in partnership with the Italian Union of the Blind and Visually Impaired of Naples. For one day, the protective barriers around the chapel’s marble masterpieces were removed so that roughly 80 participants wearing latex gloves could explore them by hand. The route included Sanmartino’s Veiled Christ, completed in 1753, and the bas-reliefs of La Pudicizia by Antonio Corradini and Il Disinganno by Francesco Queirolo.

A sculpture that defies physical explanation

The Veiled Christ has amazed viewers for centuries. Carved from a single block of white marble, it depicts Christ in death beneath a transparent shroud that appears to adhere perfectly to the body beneath, tracing the contours of the face, the lines of pain, the texture of skin. Visitors have spent centuries trying to explain it. Many still half-believe it must have involved some lost alchemy that turned real fabric into stone.

A payment receipt for Sanmartino, dated December 16, 1752, and signed by his patron Raimondo di Sangro, the Prince of Sansevero, is preserved in the Historical Archive of the Bank of Naples. Antonio Canova, one of the greatest marble sculptors of the 18th century, reportedly tried to acquire the piece and later said he would have given ten years of his life to have made something of equal quality.

What made this event different

The guides were themselves blind, trained by educator Roberta Meomartini in collaboration with the museum to describe the sculptures through touch rather than sight. The visit was free, by advance reservation only, and each participant could bring a companion or guide dog. At the end, visitors were escorted to the sacristy and given a Braille guidebook produced by the museum and the Italian Union of the Blind and Visually Impaired.

Museum president Maria Alessandra Masucci framed it as part of a longer effort: “This initiative forms part of our wider programme to create a cultural space that is inclusive and accessible through dedicated pathways and tools tailored to the different needs of museum visitors.”

The museum’s accessibility work extends beyond this event. In recent years, it has added audio guides for the visually impaired, sign language video tours for deaf visitors, and itineraries for visitors with intellectual disabilities.

Art belongs to everyone

Giuseppe Ambrosino of the Italian Union of the Blind put it directly: “Art must not be a privilege reserved for sight. Accessibility projects such as this transform a museum into a place of genuine inclusion, affirming that art belongs to everyone. In this case, visitors will not only be allowed to touch the marble sculpture; beauty itself will be able to flow through the hands and reach straight to the heart.”

The Sansevero Chapel is small, crowded with masterworks, and the Veiled Christ usually draws the most disbelieving stares. What last Tuesday offered was something harder to find: the chance to know it through your fingertips, with a sculpture that resists explanation either way.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article describes a meaningful, inclusive cultural initiative that allows blind and visually impaired visitors to experience a famous marble sculpture through touch, promoting accessibility and inclusion in the arts. The event has broad societal significance by advancing equal access to cultural heritage, is well-detailed, and represents a positive real-world impact for a marginalized group.
articles.personality_title: Naples museum lets blind visitors touch the Veiled Christ sculpture
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Veiled Christ is a famous marble statue in Naples, Italy, known for its delicate, lifelike veiling carved from stone. Normally, visitors can only see it behind protective barriers.

**What happened** – On a recent Tuesday, the museum removed barriers so about 80 blind or partially sighted visitors could touch the Veiled Christ and other sculptures. The event, called "Wonder within reach," was led by blind guides trained to describe the art through touch. Visitors wore gloves and could bring companions or guide dogs. They also received Braille guidebooks after the visit.

**Impact** – This event gave people who cannot see a rare chance to experience a masterpiece through touch, making art more accessible. The Veiled Christ’s detailed marble veil, which even experts find mysterious, could be felt directly. The museum’s work helps include people with disabilities, showing that art can be enjoyed in different ways.

**What's next step** – The museum plans to continue improving accessibility with tools like audio guides for the visually impaired and sign language tours for deaf visitors. This event is part of a broader effort to create a welcoming space for all visitors.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A Naples museum opened its doors for blind visitors to feel the Veiled Christ sculpture, making a famous artwork accessible through touch and inclusion.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un musée de Naples permet aux visiteurs aveugles de toucher le Christ voilé
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Christ voilé est une célèbre statue en marbre à Naples, Italie, connue pour son voile fin sculpté dans la pierre. Habituellement, les visiteurs ne peuvent que la voir derrière des barrières protectrices.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un mardi récent, le musée a retiré les barrières pour qu’environ 80 visiteurs aveugles ou malvoyants puissent toucher le Christ voilé et d’autres sculptures. L’événement, appelé « La merveille à portée de main », était guidé par des personnes aveugles formées à décrire l’art par le toucher. Les visiteurs portaient des gants et pouvaient venir avec un accompagnant ou un chien-guide. Ils ont aussi reçu des guides en braille après la visite.

**Impact** – Cet événement a offert aux personnes qui ne voient pas la chance rare de découvrir un chef-d’œuvre par le toucher, rendant l’art plus accessible. Le voile en marbre, très détaillé et mystérieux, pouvait être senti directement. Le travail du musée aide à inclure les personnes en situation de handicap, montrant que l’art peut être apprécié de différentes façons.

**Prochaine étape** – Le musée prévoit de continuer à améliorer l'accessibilité avec des guides audio pour les malvoyants et des visites en langue des signes pour les sourds. Cet événement fait partie d’un effort plus large pour accueillir tous les visiteurs.

**Phrase clé** – Un musée de Naples a permis aux visiteurs aveugles de toucher la sculpture du Christ voilé, rendant une œuvre célèbre accessible par le toucher et l’inclusion.
articles.personality_title_es: Museo de Nápoles permite a visitantes ciegos tocar al Cristo Velado
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Cristo Velado es una famosa estatua de mármol en Nápoles, Italia, conocida por su delicado velo tallado en piedra. Normalmente, los visitantes solo pueden verla detrás de barreras protectoras.

**Qué pasó** – Un martes reciente, el museo retiró las barreras para que unos 80 visitantes ciegos o con baja visión pudieran tocar el Cristo Velado y otras esculturas. El evento, llamado "Maravilla al alcance de la mano", fue guiado por personas ciegas entrenadas para describir el arte a través del tacto. Los visitantes usaron guantes y pudieron venir con acompañantes o perros guía. Al final recibieron guías en braille.

**Impacto** – Este evento dio a personas que no pueden ver una oportunidad rara de experimentar una obra maestra por el tacto, haciendo el arte más accesible. El velo de mármol, muy detallado y misterioso, se pudo sentir directamente. El trabajo del museo ayuda a incluir a personas con discapacidades, mostrando que el arte se puede disfrutar de diferentes maneras.

**Próximo paso** – El museo planea continuar mejorando la accesibilidad con guías de audio para personas con discapacidad visual y recorridos en lenguaje de señas para visitantes sordos. Este evento es parte de un esfuerzo mayor para crear un espacio acogedor para todos.

**Frase clave** – Un museo en Nápoles permitió a visitantes ciegos tocar la escultura del Cristo Velado, haciendo una obra famosa accesible mediante el tacto y la inclusión.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of gentle, gloved hands tenderly exploring the smooth, flowing contours of a translucent marble veil draped over a reclining figure symbolized by soft, abstract white stone shapes, set within the intimate, softly lit interior of a historic chapel, emphasizing tactile connection and inclusive accessibility.

articles.title: How Kyrgyzstan built a system to support domestic violence survivors
articles.summary: Domestic violence was not something people spoke about openly in Kyrgyzstan in the 1990s following the collapse of the Soviet Union, but after a long road of dedicated efforts, there are now laws addressing family abuse, crisis centres and hotlines while human rights defenders tackle such new challenges as sexual slavery.
articles.content:
Bübyusara Ryskulova has been on the frontlines for more than three decades. Honourary president of the crisis centre Sezim (which means “feeling”), she has worked towards helping women and children who survived violence.

While Kyrgyzstan has made significant progress in strengthening protections against domestic and family violence, challenges remain, she told UN News during the recent Commission on the Status of Women session (CSW70) at UN Headquarters in New York, where representatives from more than 100 countries discuss access to justice and the protection of women’s rights.

“My work has been connected to the very topic discussed at this session for more than 30 years,” said Ms. Ryskulova, who was the only civil society representative in a “very strong delegation” that included the deputy prime minister, ombudsperson, deputy prosecutor general, Supreme Court and various ministries.

“For me, it’s a chance to see how other countries work,” she said. “At home, you hear a lot of criticism, but still, comparison puts things into perspective.”

‘I knew how deep our problems were’

Ms. Ryskulova said she “came into this by accident”, explaining that her decision to dedicate herself to this work was inspired by a previous visit to the United States in 1998, when an exchange programme brought her to seven states to learn about ways to address domestic violence.

“I saw for the first time how a support system actually functions,” Ms. Ryskulova said. “Back home, no one even talked about these issues out loud. When they started teaching us what a public organization is, what an NGO [non-governmental organization] is, I was learning like a schoolgirl. Everything was new and very interesting. But, even then, I knew how deep our problems were.”

‘Rollback’ of women’s rights

One of the first crisis centres in the region, Sezim was founded in 1998, at a time when the very idea of helping survivors of violence was new to the country.

Even today, speaking openly about domestic violence can still be difficult, Ms. Ryskulova said.

“In the early 1990s, there’s been a noticeable rise in religiousness, and sometimes people interpret many issues through the lens of religion,” she said. “For example, in the village where I’m from, there is one school and two mosques. So, talking about violence can still be hard, and in some areas of women’s rights, there has been a certain rollback.”

Tough start

The early days were incredibly tough, especially when the first hotline was launched, she said.

“When we opened the hotline, we just sat there waiting. Will anyone call? And for a long time, no one did,” she recalled.

“When the first call finally came, they said, ‘take it’. I had almost no experience. My hands were shaking, but that’s how we started.”

UN News Bübüsara Ryskulova, honourary president of the Sezim Crisis Centre.

Progress is undeniable

Progress is undeniable. The professional landscape is slowly changing. While the country had almost no practicing psychologists in the early 1990s, now many universities train them.

After a civil society campaign and a major petition drive, in 2003, Kyrgyzstan became one of the first post‑Soviet countries to adopt a law on family violence, with legislative updates adopted in 2017.

“At the legislative level, we really can’t complain,” Ms. Ryskulova said.

“But, the law is one thing, and its implementation is another. That’s why now we work more on the ground, protecting specific people. We have a shelter, and our specialists have been working for more than 30 years. I received a second degree myself, as a practical psychologist and today I often represent survivors’ interests in court. Our law allows that, through a power of attorney, even if you’re not a lawyer.”

Sometimes, she says, these cases become high‑profile and help push the system forward.

“In recent years, our organization has handled around 40 court cases, and almost 90 per cent of the decisions were in favor of the survivors. These were very difficult cases [including] murders, rapes. We work on them, and then we raise public awareness. If a woman doesn’t want publicity, we protect her anonymity completely.”

Tackling threats like sexual slavery

International cooperation, including with UN agencies, played a major role in the development of crisis centres in Kyrgyzstan, with the International Organization for Migration (IOM) being one of their earliest partners.

“That’s where I first heard the term ‘sexual slavery’,” she said.

“Then we opened a hotline specifically for that issue. I remember one of the first girls was underage and pregnant. I met her at the airport myself. She said she didn’t want to live, that she would end her life if the baby was born. We had to take turns sitting by her hospital bed so she wouldn’t harm herself.”

Helping survivors requires daily effort

Today, Sezim continues to work with UN agencies such as the UN refugee agency, UNHCR, and UN Women.

“For us, this is very important [for] sharing expertise, training specialists [and] joint projects,” Ms. Ryskulova said. “Platforms like the Commission on the Status of Women provide the opportunity to see what is happening in the world and understand what we still need to learn,”

Despite decades of work and visible progress, she is certain about this: helping survivors still requires daily effort.

“Seminars and trainings matter and prevention matters,” she said, “but if even one person who has suffered remains without protection, then we still haven’t done everything we must.”
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article describes Kyrgyzstan's significant progress in building a support system for domestic violence survivors, including laws, crisis centers, hotlines, and court advocacy. This represents a meaningful real-world impact on women's rights and protection against family violence, with broad societal significance beyond a small niche. The story provides detailed context about the challenges, achievements, and ongoing efforts, making it a substantive and inspiring human rights advancement.
articles.personality_title: Kyrgyzstan builds stronger support for domestic violence survivors
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In the 1990s, after the Soviet Union ended, people in Kyrgyzstan rarely talked about domestic violence. There were no laws or services to help survivors, and the topic was often kept secret.

**What happened** – Over the past 30 years, thanks to activists like Bübyusara Ryskulova and organizations like the Sezim Crisis Centre, Kyrgyzstan has created laws against family violence, opened crisis centers, and set up hotlines. They also help survivors in court and work with international groups to fight issues like sexual slavery.

**Impact** – This work has changed the way Kyrgyz society handles domestic violence. For example, the country passed one of the first family violence laws in the region in 2003, and today many survivors win cases in court. The crisis centers provide safe places and psychological support, showing real progress in protecting women and children.

**What's next step** – The focus now is on making sure the laws are followed and survivors get protection every day. Training specialists and raising awareness continue, while groups like Sezim work to improve help for victims and fight new problems.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Kyrgyzstan has built a growing system of laws, services, and support to help survivors of domestic violence, turning silence into action over three decades.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Kirghizistan renforce son soutien aux victimes de violence domestique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans les années 1990, après la fin de l'Union soviétique, on parlait rarement des violences domestiques au Kirghizistan. Il n'existait ni lois ni services pour aider les victimes, et le sujet restait souvent tabou.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – En trente ans, grâce à des militantes comme Bübyusara Ryskulova et des organisations comme le centre de crise Sezim, le Kirghizistan a adopté des lois contre les violences familiales, ouvert des centres d'accueil et mis en place des lignes d'écoute. Ils assistent aussi les victimes au tribunal et coopèrent avec des groupes internationaux pour lutter contre des problèmes comme l'esclavage sexuel.

**Impact** – Ce travail a changé la manière dont la société kirghize traite les violences domestiques. Par exemple, le pays a adopté en 2003 l'une des premières lois sur les violences familiales de la région, et aujourd'hui, de nombreuses victimes gagnent leurs procès. Les centres de crise offrent des lieux sûrs et un soutien psychologique, montrant un vrai progrès dans la protection des femmes et des enfants.

**Prochaine étape** – L'objectif est maintenant de garantir l'application des lois et la protection quotidienne des victimes. La formation des spécialistes et la sensibilisation se poursuivent, tandis que des groupes comme Sezim améliorent l'aide aux victimes et combattent de nouveaux défis.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le Kirghizistan a construit un système croissant de lois, de services et de soutien pour aider les victimes de violences domestiques, transformant le silence en action en trente ans.
articles.personality_title_es: Kirguistán fortalece el apoyo a sobrevivientes de violencia doméstica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En los años 90, tras el fin de la Unión Soviética, en Kirguistán casi no se hablaba sobre la violencia doméstica. No había leyes ni servicios para ayudar a las víctimas, y el tema era un secreto.

**Qué pasó** – En 30 años, gracias a activistas como Bübyusara Ryskulova y organizaciones como el Centro de Crisis Sezim, Kirguistán creó leyes contra la violencia familiar, centros de ayuda y líneas telefónicas. También apoyan a las víctimas en los tribunales y trabajan con grupos internacionales para combatir problemas como la esclavitud sexual.

**Impacto** – Este trabajo ha cambiado la forma en que la sociedad kirguís trata la violencia doméstica. Por ejemplo, el país aprobó en 2003 una de las primeras leyes contra la violencia familiar en la región, y hoy muchas víctimas ganan sus casos. Los centros de crisis ofrecen lugares seguros y apoyo psicológico, mostrando un progreso real en la protección de mujeres y niños.

**Próximo paso** – Ahora el enfoque es asegurar que las leyes se cumplan y que las víctimas reciban protección diaria. Continúan la formación de especialistas y la concienciación, mientras grupos como Sezim mejoran la ayuda y enfrentan nuevos desafíos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Kirguistán ha construido un sistema creciente de leyes, servicios y apoyo para ayudar a sobrevivientes de violencia doméstica, convirtiendo el silencio en acción durante tres décadas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a sturdy traditional Kyrgyz yurt gently illuminated from within, surrounded by protective hands formed from soft, flowing fabric patterns, symbolizing shelter and support for survivors of domestic violence, set against a calm, natural landscape with muted earth tones and soft blues.

articles.title: Plan to scrap most short jail terms comes into effect
articles.summary: The government hopes it will ease the population crisis in prisons.
articles.content: These offenders are the most likely to leave prison and commit another crime, according to analysis - and many rehabilitation campaigners say it would be cheaper and more effective to try to turn around the lives of these people in the community.
articles.reasoning: The government's plan to scrap most short jail terms aims to reduce prison overcrowding and recidivism by focusing on community rehabilitation, which can lead to better social outcomes and more effective use of resources.
articles.personality_title: New law ends most short jail sentences to reduce prison crowding
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many prisons are overcrowded, with too many people serving short jail sentences. Studies show that people who serve short sentences often return to crime after release.

**What happened** – The government has introduced a new plan that stops most short jail sentences. Instead of going to prison, more offenders will receive help and support in their communities. This change started recently.

**Impact** – This plan aims to reduce the number of people in prison and lower the chance that offenders will commit crimes again. Experts say helping people in their communities can be cheaper and more effective than sending them to jail for a short time.

**What's next step** – Officials will watch how this change affects crime rates and prison numbers. If it works well, the government might expand community programs to support more people.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Ending most short jail terms focuses on helping offenders in the community to reduce prison crowding and repeat crimes.
articles.personality_title_fr: Nouvelle loi met fin à la plupart des courtes peines de prison pour réduire la surpopulation carcérale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreuses prisons sont surpeuplées, avec trop de personnes condamnées à de courtes peines. Des études montrent que ces personnes retournent souvent au crime après leur sortie.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Le gouvernement a lancé un plan qui supprime la plupart des courtes peines de prison. Au lieu d'aller en prison, plus de personnes recevront de l'aide et un soutien dans leur communauté. Ce changement a commencé récemment.

**Impact** – Ce plan vise à réduire le nombre de détenus et à diminuer les risques de récidive. Les experts disent qu’aider les personnes dans leur communauté peut être moins coûteux et plus efficace que de les envoyer en prison pour une courte durée.

**Prochaine étape** – Les autorités vont observer comment ce changement affecte la criminalité et la population carcérale. Si cela fonctionne bien, le gouvernement pourrait étendre les programmes communautaires pour soutenir davantage de personnes.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Mettre fin aux courtes peines de prison mise sur l’aide en communauté pour réduire la surpopulation carcérale et la récidive.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva ley elimina la mayoría de las penas cortas de prisión para reducir el hacinamiento penitenciario
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas cárceles están sobrepobladas, con demasiadas personas cumpliendo penas cortas. Los estudios muestran que estas personas a menudo vuelven a cometer delitos después de salir.

**Qué pasó** – El gobierno ha introducido un plan que elimina la mayoría de las penas cortas de prisión. En lugar de ir a la cárcel, más personas recibirán ayuda y apoyo en sus comunidades. Este cambio comenzó recientemente.

**Impacto** – Este plan busca reducir el número de personas en prisión y disminuir la posibilidad de que vuelvan a delinquir. Los expertos dicen que ayudar a las personas en sus comunidades puede ser más barato y efectivo que enviarlas a prisión por poco tiempo.

**Próximo paso** – Las autoridades observarán cómo este cambio afecta las tasas de delincuencia y la población carcelaria. Si funciona bien, el gobierno podría ampliar los programas comunitarios para apoyar a más personas.

**Frase resumen** – El fin de la mayoría de las penas cortas de cárcel se enfoca en ayudar a los delincuentes en la comunidad para reducir el hacinamiento y la reincidencia.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of an open gate symbolizing release, with a winding path leading through a peaceful village where diverse, stylized keys gently float upward like birds, representing freedom and new opportunities through community support and rehabilitation.
